title: Why Logan Square's boulevard still sets the tone
dek: The wide, tree-lined boulevard that gives the neighborhood its shape is also why it still feels unhurried, even as everything around it changes.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Most people find Logan Square through its restaurants first and its architecture second, if at all. That is a mistake, because the boulevard is the reason everything else works.

Logan, Kedzie, and Humboldt Boulevards form a wide green spine that was designed for slow movement long before anyone worried about that on purpose. The result today is a neighborhood where a Saturday can move from the farmers market at the square, to a bench under the monument, to a coffee shop two blocks off the main drag, without ever feeling like a commute between destinations.

New residents often ask whether the neighborhood's popularity has priced out its original character. The honest answer is that it has changed the retail mix more than the built environment. The greystone two-flats and courtyard buildings that define most blocks are largely intact, protected in part by the same boulevard frontage rules that shaped the area a century ago.

For anyone relocating, the practical takeaway is this: proximity to the boulevard itself commands a premium, but blocks two or three off it, still walkable to the same restaurants and the Blue Line, offer meaningfully more house for the money.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Logan Boulevard at Logan Square, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Kasama, and the case for eating well in the West Loop before you buy
dek: A Michelin-starred Filipino breakfast counter turned tasting menu, and what it tells you about the neighborhood's ambitions.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Kasama started as a daytime bakery and breakfast counter and became the first Filipino restaurant in the country to earn a Michelin star, eventually converting entirely to an evening tasting menu built around long-fermented rice, live-fire cooking, and a wine list that takes itself seriously without being precious about it.

What it says about the West Loop is more useful than the meal itself, if you are relocating and trying to read a neighborhood's trajectory. A restaurant like this does not open on a whim; it follows density, foot traffic, and a customer base willing to wait for a table on a Tuesday. The West Loop has had that combination for close to a decade now, and the restaurant scene has only gotten more ambitious, not less.

For a prospective buyer, that ambition is a reasonable proxy for how a neighborhood will feel in five years, not just today. Fulton Market's restaurant density is not going to thin out, and the new-construction pipeline nearby is priced accordingly.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Fulton-Randolph Market District, via Wikimedia Commons

title: What to expect at Andersonville's Midsommarfest
dek: A two-day street festival that leans into the neighborhood's Swedish roots without feeling like a theme park version of them.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Midsommarfest closes down Clark Street through the heart of Andersonville for a weekend each June, built around the same Scandinavian midsummer tradition the neighborhood has marked for decades, alongside the more familiar street-festival staples of live music stages and food vendors from the surrounding blocks.

For anyone new to the city, it is a useful, low-stakes way to get a feel for a neighborhood before committing to it. You will walk the same stretch of Clark Street you would use for groceries and dinner on any ordinary week, just with a stage at one end and a beer garden at the other.

Because it draws from well beyond Andersonville itself, parking gets difficult fast. The Red Line to Berwyn or Argyle is the more reliable way in, and it also happens to be the same commute you would have if you moved here.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Andersonville Commercial Historic District, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Bronzeville's greystones are finally getting their due
dek: A wave of restoration is bringing back the rowhouses that once defined the center of Black Chicago's cultural life.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: In the early twentieth century, Bronzeville was the center of gravity for Black Chicago, a role it held through the Great Migration and the decades that followed, and the greystone rowhouses along its boulevards were built to match that stature.

Many of those buildings sat underinvested for decades after the neighborhood's economic base shifted. What has changed more recently is the scale of restoration work, block by block, bringing individual greystones back to something closer to their original condition rather than replacing them outright.

For buyers, the appeal is straightforward: architecture and boulevard-scale planning that would be unaffordable in Lincoln Park or the Gold Coast, in a neighborhood with a train line into downtown and a strong claim on the years ahead as reinvestment continues.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Ida B. Wells National Monument, Bronzeville, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Girl & the Goat, a decade in, still explains the West Loop's pull
dek: Stephanie Izard's flagship remains the reservation that's hardest to get in the neighborhood that made reservations hard to get.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Girl & the Goat opened in 2010, before Randolph Street was a destination in its own right, and its early success is one of the reasons the rest of the block filled in behind it. The menu is built around shared plates and open-fire cooking, and the restaurant remains one of the harder same-week reservations in the city more than a decade later.

For someone relocating and trying to gauge whether a neighborhood's food scene has staying power or is a temporary wave, longevity like this is a better signal than any single new opening. The West Loop's restaurant identity was not an accident of one developer's plan; it has been reinforced by repeat success for well over a decade.
If you're neighborhood-hunting on a Friday night, a walk-by of Randolph and Green will tell you more about the area's current energy than most listing photos will.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Fulton Market, West Loop, via Wikimedia Commons

title: The Air and Water Show is still the best free reason to live near the lake
dek: Two days each August when the Blue Angels fly directly over North Side lakefront neighborhoods, for free.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Each August, the Chicago Air and Water Show draws close to a million people to the lakefront between North Avenue and Montrose, with military and civilian aerobatic teams performing directly over the water. It remains free, which is unusual for an event of its scale.

The best vantage points are exactly the lakefront neighborhoods you would be evaluating anyway: Lincoln Park's beaches, Belmont Harbor, and the stretch up through Uptown all get a direct view without a ticket or a rooftop deck.

If you are touring homes in these neighborhoods in the summer, ask what the building's roof access situation is. On Air and Water Show weekend, that detail matters more than almost anything else in the listing.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Chicago Air and Water Show, via Wikimedia Commons

title: You can walk past three Frank Lloyd Wright houses on one Beverly block
dek: The city's hilliest neighborhood is also its most architecturally dense outside of downtown, and most of it is hiding in plain sight on residential streets.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Beverly sits on the Blue Island Ridge, the highest natural elevation in the city, and the terrain shaped the way its early developers platted the neighborhood; the result is a street grid with actual slopes, curves, and sightlines you will not find anywhere else in Chicago.

That same era of development coincided with a wave of Prairie School commissions, including several homes designed by Frank Lloyd Wright and his contemporary Walter Burley Griffin, most of which remain private residences on otherwise ordinary residential blocks.

The practical upshot for a relocating family is a neighborhood that reads as suburban in lot size and quiet, while still sitting inside city limits with a direct Metra connection downtown, and a level of architectural pedigree usually associated with far pricier North Side addresses.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL

title: Taste of Chicago is a crash course in the city's food geography
dek: One weekend in Grant Park gives you a faster tour of the city's restaurant landscape than months of scattered dinners would.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Taste of Chicago began in 1980 and remains one of the largest free food festivals in the country, bringing dozens of restaurants from across the city into Grant Park for a long weekend, typically anchored by a concert lineup on the Petrillo Music Shell stage.

For someone new to the city, the honest value is less about any single dish and more about the map it draws for you: a booth from a Pilsen taqueria next to one from a Lincoln Square German restaurant next to one from a South Side rib joint gives you a faster sense of the city's food geography than months of scattered dinners would.

The festival sits inside the South Loop, walkable from the Museum Campus and a short ride from just about anywhere on the Red, Green, or Orange Lines, which makes it one of the easier big Chicago events to attend without a car.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Grant Park, Chicago, via Wikimedia Commons

title: West Loop or Fulton Market? They're closer than the real estate listings suggest
dek: The two names get used almost interchangeably now, but there's a real difference in what you're buying into.
author: Jason Hinsley
body: Fulton Market is technically the northern edge of the West Loop, a former meatpacking corridor along Fulton Street that has been rebranded hard enough over the last decade to function as its own address in most listings.

The distinction that actually matters for a buyer is building age and use, not geography. Fulton Market proper is dominated by new-construction and adaptive-reuse buildings, several with hotel or office space mixed into the same block, while the wider West Loop still has more of the two- and three-flat housing stock you would expect from a residential Chicago neighborhood.
Neither is more correct than the other; they are simply two different products at two different price points, sitting a five-minute walk apart.
authorTitle: Founder & Designated Managing Broker, RE/MAX Metropolitan IL
photoCredit: Randolph Street, Chicago, 1880, via Wikimedia Commons
